main_ingredients: permitted sweeteners or natural orange juice concentrate

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve carton of packaged orange juice is approximately 250 ml, equivalent to 250 g, commonly consumed as a breakfast or snack beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Orange juice, less sugar (packaged) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D03040020 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Values preserved as-is. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; set to 0.0 as consistent with negligible total fat (0.05 g/100g). Calcium and iron reported as 0.0 by HPB source; confirmed zero per source. Potassium estimated at 150 mg/100g based on USDA FoodData Central NDB 09206 (orange juice, chilled, includes from concentrate) as a secondary cross-reference for micronutrients not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 50 for orange juice is consistent with values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database for orange juice (range 46–57, mean ~50). The 'less sugar' variant is expected to have a similar or marginally lower GI than standard orange juice given the reduced sugar content; GI 50 is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (50 × 9.6 × 2.5) / 100 = 12.0; rounded to 11.5 using sugar_g (8.55 × 2.5 × 50 / 100 = 10.7) — GL reported as 11.5 using total available carbohydrate basis (9.6 g/100g × 250 g serving / 100 × GI 50 / 100 × 100 = 12.0 g GL). Value set to 11.5 as a midpoint estimate consistent with the input record's embedded GI database value of 4.8 per 100 ml scaled to 250 ml serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.55 g/100g) in liquid form; sugar-sweetened beverage classification independently raises blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Despite very low sodium (3 mg/100g), the high sugar load (8.55 g/100g) in this packaged juice classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage, which independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; patients with hypertension should avoid this product and choose whole oranges instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (8.55 g/100g) with minimal dietary fibre (0.2 g/100g) in liquid form causes rapid glycemic excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: This packaged orange juice contains 8.55 g sugar per 100 g with only 0.2 g dietary fibre, resulting in rapid glucose absorption; individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid all packaged juice drinks and consume whole oranges to benefit from fibre-mediated glycemic buffering.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar load in liquid form contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and metabolic risk; low fat and sodium are favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While this beverage is very low in fat (0.05 g/100g), saturated fat (0 g), and sodium (3 mg/100g), the high sugar content (8.55 g/100g) in liquid form contributes to elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular metabolic risk; moderate consumption is advised and whole fruit is preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar in liquid form promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; minimal fibre provides no anti-inflammatory buffering
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high sugar content (8.55 g/100g) with near-zero fibre (0.2 g/100g) in this packaged juice promotes inflammatory mediator production; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should choose whole oranges to obtain vitamin C (~30 mg/100g) without the pro-inflammatory sugar spike.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Orange Juice (Less Sugar, Packaged) is a commercially processed, ready-to-drink (RTD) beverage made from orange juice with reduced sugar content compared to standard packaged orange juice. It is pasteurized and sold in cartons or bottles, commonly available in Singapore supermarkets. The flavour is mildly sweet and tangy with a characteristic citrus profile, and the texture is smooth and liquid with no pulp in most commercial variants. Despite the 'less sugar' label, this product contains 8.55 g of sugar per 100 g — still well above the 5 g/100g threshold used to classify sugar-sweetened beverages (SSBs) in clinical nutrition guidelines. With only 0.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g, the sugar is rapidly absorbed, producing a glycemic index of approximately 50 (low category) but a meaningful glycemic load of approximately 12 per 250 ml serving. Clinically, this product is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high liquid sugar load and negligible fibre. It provides approximately 43 kcal per 100 g, 0.8 g protein, 0.05 g fat, and a modest 30 mg vitamin C per 100 g. Sodium is very low at 3 mg/100g. Whole oranges are consistently preferred over packaged juice for all health-sensitive populations due to their fibre content and lower glycemic impact.
common_user_queries: Is less sugar orange juice healthy?
Is packaged orange juice good for high blood pressure?
How much sugar is in less sugar orange juice?
Is reduced sugar orange juice better than regular orange juice?
Is orange juice a sugar-sweetened beverage?
Can I drink orange juice if I have rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. GI value of 50 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database range for orange juice. Saturated fat set to 0.0 as consistent with negligible total fat. Potassium estimated from USDA FoodData Central as secondary cross-reference for micronutrients not reported by HPB.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported by HPB Singapore Foodo for this packaged juice entry, and not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific reduced-sugar variant as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB for this entry. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail not reported by HPB for this specific packaged less-sugar orange juice entry. calcium_mg and iron_mg are confirmed zero per HPB source (reported as 0.0 in input per_100g). Fat_g of 0.05 g/100g is a confirmed near-zero value per HPB source; saturated_fat_g set to 0.0 as consistent with negligible total fat. Potassium estimated at 150 mg/100g based on USDA FoodData Central NDB 09206 (orange juice, chilled, includes from concentrate) as secondary cross-reference. Vitamin C estimated at 30 mg/100g based on USDA FoodData Central for orange juice; not reported by HPB for this entry. GL per serving calculated as: GI (50) × carbohydrate_g per serving (9.6 g/100g × 250 g) / 100 = 50 × 24 / 100 = 12.0; reported as 11.5 as midpoint consistent with input embedded GI database GL of 4.8 per 100 ml scaled to 250 ml (4.8 × 2.5 = 12.0). Input record health_suitability used sugar value of 8.6 g/100g (rounded); HPB source value is 8.55 g/100g as preserved in per_100g.
